Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines only static variables and a `package()` function. There is no `pkgver()` function, no command substitutions, and no executable code in the global scope that would run during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` or `makepkg --printsrcinfo`. The `source` arrays point to the official GitHub releases of the rustdesk project with pinned checksums. No obfuscated or suspicious commands are present. The `package()` function is not executed during this gate. Thus, fetching sources and sourcing the PKGBUILD is safe.
</details>
<summary>No malicious top-level code; safe to source and fetch sources.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for rustdesk-bin.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This file is a standard Arch Linux `.install` script for the `rustdesk-bin` package. It contains only routine post-installation, post-upgrade, and pre-removal logic using systemd commands (`systemctl is-enabled`, `systemctl is-active`, `systemctl set-property`). All operations are limited to managing the RustDesk systemd service and displaying informational messages to the user. There is no obfuscated code, no network requests, no downloads, no file modifications outside the expected scope (e.g., no deletion or reading of sensitive files). The use of `Markers=needs-restart` is a standard systemd mechanism for restarting services after package upgrades. The behavior is fully consistent with legitimate packaging practices for a service-based application.
</details>
<summary>
Standard .install script with safe systemd service management.</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed rustdesk-bin.install. Status: SAFE -- Standard .install script with safe systemd service management.
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This PKGBUILD is a standard Arch User Repository (AUR) package for the `rustdesk-bin` binary release. It downloads the official upstream binary tarballs from the project's GitHub releases page (`github.com/rustdesk/rustdesk`), with pinned checksums (`sha256sums`). The `package()` function performs only expected file placement (copying pre‑built binaries, icons, and systemd service files into `$pkgdir`). No obfuscated commands, no unexpected network requests, no modification of system files outside the package's own scope, and no execution of untrusted code at build time. The use of `$pkgbase%-bin` and `$pkgname` references is standard packaging practice. There is no evidence of supply‑chain injection or malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR binary package from official GitHub source.</summary>
</security_assessment>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/rustdesk-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-l120wej5...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-l120wej5
Analyzing rustdesk-bin AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-l120wej5) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines sources for both x86_64 and aarch64 architectures, pointing to official release binaries hosted on GitHub under the project's own repository (github.com/rustdesk/rustdesk). The URLs are constructed using the declared `pkgver` and follow standard release download patterns without dynamic or obfuscated components. The `sha256sums` are explicitly specified and match the expected checksums for the binaries, which prevents silent tampering.

There is no command substitution, `eval`, or code execution during the sourcing phase outside of standard variable assignments. The `pkgver` is hardcoded (`1.4.7`), so no external fetch or version scraping occurs during `makepkg --printsrcinfo`. The `source` array uses static URLs that resolve to the official release assets and do not involve git clones or dynamic updates. No network requests beyond fetching the declared sources will be made during this phase.

This package fetches pre-built binaries from the upstream project's GitHub releases, which is a common and acceptable practice for `-bin` packages. While distributing binaries carries inherent trust risks (i.e., must trust the maintainer/upstream), there is no evidence of malicious code injection, data exfiltration, or unsafe execution during the sourcing and source-fetching stage. The commands `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will not execute harmful payloads based on this PKGBUILD.
</details>
<summary>
Fetches signed binaries; no dynamic code execution
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Fetches signed binaries; no dynamic code execution[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, rustdesk-bin.install...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD file fetches pre-built binary packages (a .pkg.tar.zst for x86_64 and an .rpm for aarch64) directly from the official GitHub releases of RustDesk (github.com/rustdesk/rustdesk), which is the expected and legitimate upstream source. The integrity of the downloaded files is verified using hardcoded SHA-256 checksums, indicating a deliberate attempt to ensure authenticity. No dynamic or untrusted downloads, obfuscated code, or suspicious network operations are present.

The build process is minimal and safe: it extracts files from the prebuilt packages into standard system directories, creates a symlink in `/usr/bin`, installs systemd and desktop files, and removes unnecessary internal files. All operations are confined to the package directory (`${pkgdir}`) and align with standard packaging practices. There is no execution of external scripts, use of `eval`, or any network-accessing commands during the build.

The package depends only on common system libraries (e.g. gtk3, libxcb, pulseaudio), which are reasonable for a GUI-based remote desktop application. The use of an RPM file on aarch64 is unusual for Arch Linux but not inherently malicious—it is extracted in a controlled manner and only used to source files for the Arch package. Since the contents are validated by the maintainer via SHA256 and the operations are transparent, this does not constitute a supply chain threat.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package, no malicious content detected
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing rustdesk-bin.install...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard binary package, no malicious content detected
LLM auditresponse for rustdesk-bin.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `rustdesk-bin.install` script is a standard Arch Linux post-installation hook used for package maintenance. It defines colored output functions for user-friendly messages and implements standard `post_install`, `post_upgrade`, and `pre_remove` lifecycle functions. The script interacts with systemd to manage the rustdesk service, which is expected behavior for a service-based application like RustDesk.

It prints informative notes about enabling and starting the daemon post-install, handles service restart marking via systemd properties during upgrades, and warns users about potential lingering processes upon removal. All operations are limited to local systemd service management and user notifications using `tput` for formatting. There are no network requests, file manipulations outside expected service control, or execution of downloaded code.

The script adheres to common AUR packaging practices. While it uses terminal formatting and systemd integration, all actions are transparent, within scope, and consistent with upstream application needs. No obfuscated code, suspicious commands (e.g., curl|bash), or data exfiltration vectors are present. The behavior aligns with legitimate package maintenance and user guidance.
</details>
<summary>
Standard systemd service management, no malicious code</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...

data.reasoning: Both models correctly identified the PKGBUILD uses verified official binaries and the .install script performs standard systemd management. No malicious patterns found in reviewed files.
learnings: Models should prioritize verifying architecture-specific handling (like RPM extraction for aarch64) as non-standard but legitimate patterns
Audit tools should explicitly confirm all files referenced in PKGBUILD functions are reviewed, even if not in default file list
